narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 36 and plans to attend full-time, you've put real effort into your law school application, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.60 and middle-income family background. The admissions model we use predicted a 55.6% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold that leads to rejecting applications to protect the school's outcomes. This prediction came from weighing various parts of your profile against patterns from thousands of past students. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is much lower than the typical student's rank of around the 5th or 6th decile—meaning your later academic performance placed you near the bottom of your peers, a strong signal historically linked to bar struggles. Next in influence, your LSAT score worked strongly in your favor, as it's close to the average of 36.7 and pulled the prediction toward expecting you to pass. Your middle family income bracket had a smaller but negative effect, nudging the outlook slightly toward failure compared to higher-income peers who often have more resources for exam prep. Overall, your lower Year 3 grades outweighed the positives from your LSAT and stood out as the top driver when compared to typical admitted students, who tend to maintain stronger decile rankings throughout. While your full-time status aligns with most successful applicants, it didn't shift the balance much here. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man pursuing law—many in similar circumstances succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic deciles through stronger coursework or retakes, perhaps seeking mentorship or prep programs tailored for underrepresented students. Consider retaking the LSAT if possible to push even higher, and gather letters highlighting your resilience and community impact. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could flip the model's view in your favor.
